---
title: "Interpretable Rule-Based Models for Multiclass Classification"
author: "Prof. Dr. Robin Nunkesser"
format: revealjs
---

# DATA 2026

## Context

- Focus: interpretable multiclass classification
- Data type: tabular data
- Model families: rule sets, rule lists, decision trees
- Goal: bridge communities and model spaces

## Why This Topic?

- Interpretability is critical in high-stakes settings
- Multiclass tasks are common and often harder to explain
- Research is spread across separate communities
- Cross-community integration is still limited

# Interpretable Hypothesis Spaces

## Rule Set

- Unordered collection of rules
- Multiple matching rules can fire for one instance
- Prediction via voting or score aggregation
- Default rule when no rule matches

## Rule List

- Ordered sequence of rules
- First matching rule determines prediction
- Compact and easy to read
- Limited flexibility compared to rule sets

## Decision Tree

- Hierarchical sequence of feature tests
- Leaves define class prediction
- Highly intuitive visualization
- Can become large without optimization

## Multiclass Handling

- Generic strategy: One-vs-All or One-vs-One
- Problem: often reduces direct interpretability
- Better: native multiclass models
- Rule sets can use class-wise score aggregation

## Scoring View

- Each fired rule contributes class scores
- Example notation: $p_c \mathrel{+}= v$
- Predict class with highest cumulative score
- Same concept can also enrich lists and trees

# Comparison of Model Families

## Strengths and Trade-offs

| Family | Main strength | Main limitation |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Rule sets | Flexible decision boundaries | Contradictions and uncovered instances possible |
| Rule lists | Very readable decision flow | Less expressive than full rule sets |
| Decision trees | Intuitive global structure | Can grow large and less compact |

## Key Structural Relation

- Rule sets can represent rule lists
- Rule sets can represent decision trees
- Reverse direction is possible but may replicate structure
- This motivates a shared representational view

# Cross-Community Perspective

## Potential Siloing

- Methods originate in ML, data mining, bioinformatics, EC
- Citation links are uneven across communities
- Evolutionary and non-evolutionary lines are weakly connected
- Integration opportunities are substantial

## Why This Matters

- Strong methods may be overlooked across communities
- Benchmarks and tooling differ by community
- Comparability and transfer suffer
- Unified model-space thinking can reduce fragmentation

# Experimental Comparison (Preprint)

## Selection Criteria

- Native multiclass capability
- Open-source availability
- Explicit interpretability goal

## Setup

- UCI benchmark datasets (binary and multiclass)
- Repeated runs with different random seeds
- Metrics: F1 score and model size proxy
- Runtime discussed qualitatively

## Main Observations

- OneR and Slipper underperform in our runs
- CART often produces larger models
- HS can reduce tree size while preserving quality
- RuleKit and logicGP show strong performance-interpretability balance

# Toward Scored Rule Sets

## Unifying Idea

- Use scored rule sets as a common hypothesis space
- Represent and compare models from different families uniformly
- Enable transformations across algorithm outputs
- Retain interpretability with competitive performance

## Practical Outlook

- Better compaction for evolutionary rule systems
- Broader tooling integration (for example scikit-learn style interfaces)
- Shared package for scored-rule-set workflows
- Stronger cross-community benchmarking standards

# Takeaways

## Summary

- Interpretable multiclass learning is feasible and competitive
- Rule sets, rule lists, and trees are deeply connected
- Cross-community siloing is real but addressable
- Scored rule sets provide a promising unifying direction

## Thank you

- DATA 2026
- Questions and discussion welcome
